ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers cooked rice noodles as the base; no composite dish code exists for spicy pork intestine rice noodle in FoodEx2. Broader code used as the primary starch component anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Spicy Pork Intestine Rice Noodle is approximately 400 g, as typically served at Singaporean Chinese noodle stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Pork Intestine Rice Noodle 红烧肥肠小锅米线 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spicy Pork Intestine Rice Noodle (cr_id G00010510) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and detailed vitamins not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice noodles, cooked (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Pork Intestine Rice Noodle as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from cooked rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The protein (6.5 g/100g), fat (4.21 g/100g), and fibre (3.7 g/100g) content of the dish are expected to moderately attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain rice noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 100.76) / 100 ≈ 61.5, where carbohydrate per 400g serving = 25.19 × 4 = 100.76 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1.0)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to one small bowl (≤300 g) and request reduced doubanjiang and soy sauce to keep sodium intake below 500 mg per serving; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial DASH-diet benefit but does not eliminate the need for portion control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles carry a medium GI (~61); carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g partially mitigated by 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 200–250 g per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic load, and monitor postprandial glucose, as the medium GI of rice noodles combined with a large serving can produce a significant glycemic excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork intestine is an organ meat high in saturated fat and arachidonic acid; sodium 231 mg/100g adds cardiovascular load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently (no more than once per week), keep portions small (≤200 g), and request reduced chili oil and doubanjiang to limit sodium and saturated fat intake, as pork intestine contributes to LDL elevation and systemic inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork intestine is a pro-inflammatory organ meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); chili and doubanjiang are nightshade-derived
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish or substitute pork intestine with a lean protein, as the organ meat's high arachidonic acid and saturated fat content amplify inflammatory cytokine production, and nightshade-derived chili may exacerbate joint sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Pork Intestine Rice Noodle (红烧肥肠小锅米线, Hóng Shāo Féi Cháng Xiǎo Guō Mǐ Xiàn) is a Chinese-Singaporean noodle dish featuring braised pork intestine served in a spicy, aromatic broth over smooth rice vermicelli (米线). The dish is prepared by slow-braising cleaned pork intestine in a sauce of doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and chili until tender and deeply flavoured, then ladling the braised intestine and broth over freshly boiled rice noodles in a small individual pot or bowl. The texture is rich and gelatinous from the collagen-rich intestine, with a bold, spicy, and umami-forward flavour profile. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, and 327 mg potassium. The fibre content is notable for a noodle dish and comes from the rice noodles and vegetable aromatics. The dish carries a medium glycemic index (~61) due to the rice noodle base, and the glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is approximately 61.5. Clinically, the sodium content warrants portion control for hypertensive patients, while the medium GI and moderate carbohydrate load require monitoring for Type 2 Diabetes patients. Pork intestine is high in saturated fat and arachidonic acid, making this dish a concern for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. The dish is not halal and contains soy allergens from doubanjiang and soy sauce. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore's Chinese food stalls and restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010510) with a recorded confidence of 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (cooked rice noodles, University of Sydney GI Database) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g set to -1: HPB Singapore Foodo does not report saturated fat for this entry (G00010510); detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in the HPB database for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this entry. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: micronutrient detail not reported by HPB for this entry. 4. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: trace mineral detail not reported by HPB for this entry. 5. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. 6. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is the HPB-reported value and is accepted as confirmed zero per the approved source, though pork intestine is known to contain cholesterol in other databases — the HPB value may reflect a composite dish dilution effect or a reporting convention; this is noted as a data anomaly for future review. 7. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from cooked rice noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 8. Ontology codes mapped to broader classes; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this specific composite dish. 9.
